pick.rationale: Michigan State holds a significant advantage in health and tournament pedigree against a Louisville squad missing starting point guard Mikel Brown Jr., their #2 scorer and top facilitator who has been sidelined for four games with a reaggravated back injury. result.text: Spartans sliding in NCAA NET, KenPom rankings following recent losing skid Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Women's Basketball Hockey Tom Izzo Recruiting Big Ten Sports Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports MSU basketball sliding in NCAA NET, KenPom rankings amid recent losing skid Robert Bondy Spartans Wire It has been a rough stretch of games for the Spartans recently, which has resulted in them starting to slip in two of the most notable rating systems in college basketball. Michigan State has lost three of their last four games, with the most recent defeat coming at Wisconsin in blowout fashion. The Spartans trailed essentially the entire game en route to a 20-plus point loss to the Badgers. With the loss to Wisconsin, Michigan State is now 20-5 overall and 10-4 in Big Ten play. The Spartans are still looking like a lock to reach the NCAA Tournament this year, but how has the recent slump impacted their ratings in KenPom and NET? Well, they've started to slide in both. Through games on Saturday, Michigan State sits at No. 13 in the KenPom rankings . For NET, Michigan State is a few spots lower at No. 15 in those rankings . Michigan State will look to get things back on track next week with a pair of home games ahead. The Spartans will first play UCLA on Tuesday night before Ohio State comes to town on Sunday. The Spartans could really use wins in both of those games to help bolster their NCAA Tournament resume and start moving back up the rankings. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

The freshman, who's projected to be an NBA lottery pick later this year, is the Louisville Cardinals' No. 2 scorer and top facilitator. Will Brown return for UofL's first-round NCAA Tournament game Thursday vs. South Florida? Here's the latest update. Will Louisville basketball 's starting point guard, Mikel Brown Jr. , return from a reaggravated back injury to play in its NCAA Tournament opener against South Florida ? Coach Pat Kelsey didn't know for certain when he met with reporters Monday morning, three days before the sixth-seeded Cardinals battle the No. 11 Bulls in a 1:30 p.m. tipoff Thursday at KeyBank Center in Buffalo, New York: "He made great progress last week," Kelsey said. "We'll see how the next couple of days of practice go." Brown, a freshman who's projected to be an NBA lottery pick later this year, has been sidelined for UofL's past four games — up to 12 total on the season. Kelsey said he reaggravated the back injury, which initially surfaced in mid-December, by taking some hard falls during a Feb. 23 loss at then-No. 19 North Carolina . Last Wednesday at the ACC Tournament , Brown told reporters he was "starting to get there. I'm slowly progressing; I'm slowly doing more day in and day out, just trying to get a feel for it. Brown Louisville Courier Journal Show Caption Hide Caption Louisville basketball's Mikel Brown Jr. provides back injury update Mikel Brown Jr. breaks down the back injury keeping him out of the ACC Tournament and gives an update on his recovery for the NCAA Tournament. Louisville basketball received a No. 6 seed in the NCAA Tournament on Selection Sunday. The Cardinals rated well in many key metrics, but they went just 7-5 in games Mikel Brown Jr. missed due to injury. UofL will need its star point guard healthy to navigate its challenging path for a deep March Madness run. Louisville basketball , in a season of many what-ifs, just had another on Selection Sunday with the NCAA’s release of the tournament bracket . The Cardinals received a No. 6 seed in the East Region and will face No. 11-seed and American Conference champion South Florida on Thursday at 1:30 p.m. in the first round. That’s at least one seed line higher than they should be. As much as the fate of how long they last in the nation’s best championship tournament will be determined by the availability of star freshman guard Mikel Brown Jr. , their position as a 6-seed can be attributed to him too. Through no fault of his own, the back injury that forced Brown to miss 10 regular-season games and kept him sidelined for the entire ACC Tournament cost UofL from having a better seed and a slightly easier path to advancing. Brown has done everything he can to play, even trying to play hurt through the pain. After he did so in UofL's loss to Clemson, coach Pat Kelsey said it was best to shut him down until he could come back fully healthy. Brown told The Courier Journal after Louisville's ACC Tournament win over SMU that it's been a childhood dream to play in the NCAA Tournament, which he called "the best basketball (atmosphere) in the world." "I'm not going to just sit out for no reason," Brown said. "I want to play with these guys, I want to be out there, I want to play for the team, but my health is first." Brown's absence was costly in terms of the hypothetical games the Cards perhaps could have won had he played. And it was costly in terms of the reality that his status was a topic of conversation by the NCAA Selection Committee when it began constructing the bracket.
